body.notes: Participation in IETF meetings constitutes agreement to the Note Well, which permits recording, transcription, and public archival of proceedings. See also BCP 78 (RFC 5378) and BCP 79 (RFC 8179) for IPR policies.

body.title: Extensions to Salted Challenge Response (SCRAM) for 2 factor authentication
Chair's slides and update on SCRAM 2FA draft

sassle scram and then for some reason reason the connection gets interrupted so the client has to reconnect and we want to make it fast uh want to make the reconnection fast so what the client does is it deres the hash token of the initiator that's the hmac construct we see here and sends it to the server the server is able to verify um this hash token and does the same with a a small modification for the key um instead of initiator the conat responder to the channel binding data and in in its answer the server sends back the hash token for the responder and the client is now also able to verify um the result and if both verifications are successful then we have successfully authenticated the connection and both sides have been mutually authentify um what's the current status of thep uh of the ID um So within the xmpp community we saw a lot of traction of the C fast um um extension protocol and this extension protocol uses sassle HT therefore cassle HD has seen wide adoption I would call it I think most major xmpp software Stacks implemented it in one way or another so I'm pretty confident that it's um it has been a success so far it's still an experiment to some degree nevertheless um and in the latest um capap I um there was a in the latest version of the sassle HD draft there was a change to the wire protocol basically the responder message can now signal an error condition um this was added to be in par with existing sassle mechanisms which all have this error condition signaling which sassle HT lacked um so now the question is BAS basically how how should we deal with this wire format change given there are already deployments um we could renam the mechanism to ht2 we could ignore that and expect implementations to do a uh a fallback to the old or new wire format maybe we can do something else all in all there are not many open questions at least for my side and at least um that's the feeling I have talking to various people who implemented it um the only outstanding fashion from from my view is a little thing called the zero RT data so TLS 1.3 as many of you probably are aware introduced the zero round data and while I'm not aware of any implementation actually using it I don't think it's sensible that we close the door forces instead we should try to design our protocols in a way that zero round trip data can be used after all that's the ultimate goal to speed up the re um reconnection process and zero RT data is a can be can help with that um but the question is when it comes to zero round trip data is what should be contained in this zero round trip data and sule HT and um the instant streamer Samsung App requires the TLs round data to only contain the data that is required for authentication while accept fast and um others um went a different route where they also show of further data besides the data that is re required for authentication uh into the zero round trip data and it's debatable how secure this is but yeah that's an open discussion uh we had a discussion on the xmpp mailing list but we did not reach a conclusion yet so with that I thank you for your attention and I'm happy to ask open any questions you may have uh to answer any questions you may have okay art did you want to uh comment on this absolutely uh looks neat however it seems to me that this is very strongly tied to a single TCP connection meaning it um seems a little in conflict with happy eyeballs and with the multi multiple connections with the several connections that many male clients use for example it's reasonably common for one male client to open a connection authenticate and then just enable idle mode and listen for changes and use another connection to do everything yeah um what happens no my question first what happens if you try to open two or three connections at exactly the same time with this has this been considered um no uh this hasn't been considered but that's basically the question how quickly you burn the token right so that's seems to be a question that's up to the application protocol how quickly the token is invalidated is it invalidated after it's been used once or is it invalidated after uh five minutes after it has been used once if you do the letter then you can use sassle HT for the scenarios you described pretty well I think um you can create multiple connections authenticate using sule H HD with the same token um um forces multiple connection and have keep them established another way of course would be and that's also part of the application protocol specific extension how many tokens you hand to the client in this example there's only one token given to the client but you could also give three tokens to the client and then it could use so three tokens to authenticate three or to yeah to authenticate three connections right um I think you're saying that there's there are no uh deep security properties that depend on invalidating the co the token after a single usage is that correct um doing like a minute or five minutes is yeah that's exactly what we are currently experimenting this um how long should the token be valid um yeah exactly that's that's that's an open question for experimentation I'd say um I I saw that U Matthew wild is in the um participants list um he's an xmpp zaro developer I could imagine that he has an opinion on that but besides that there is no hard constraint yet yeah in xmpp implementations we're currently rotating daily I think by default um but yeah I don't think you have to change it immediately on usage I think from a security standpoint maybe graning multiple tokens if you want to establish or reauthenticate multiple connection at once is maybe a little bit more secure but that's just a gut feeling of mine okay um any FAL questions Alex say uh we are over time so I'm going to email my answers well questions about renaming the mechanism and why you would would want to do one way or the other um I was trying I have a slight uh suggesting next steps but we really kind of run out of time I'm wondering whether people are interested in continuing discussion on remaining list and maybe having an interim in a month's time or so um can I get a feel of the room both local and remote if people are interested in interim um can you repeat the question once more uh I'm suggesting uh because we're run out of time and there seem to be enough interest in this I suggest an interim uh online meeting uh in a month's time and Stephen suggests maybe a bit more than a month's time yep sounds good sounds good um all right I'll send email to the mailing list and and try to um see how much interest um and just a quick note so we have the pass key discussion which is unresolved I think that needs to continue there is also a separate discussion about a quickly fast re authentication mechanism which are useful independently on whether or not we'll work on Pass key because there are other things that can still be faster authentication mechanisms can be used with other authentications Su mechanisms other than Pass key so uh we have some examples of this okay thank you very much for coming and uh I'll try mail the mailing list on next steps all right thank you all thank you right supp
